Questions.Title: Definition of a database
Questions.QuestionText: Which of the following best defines a database?
Questions.Hint: Think about what makes a database different from a simple list of data.
Questions.Feedback: A database is an organized collection of structured data stored and managed so it can be easily accessed, updated, and retrieved — far more than a simple list or file.

Options.Text: An organized collection of structured data stored and managed for easy access, updating, and retrieval
A collection of unformatted text documents stored on a server
A spreadsheet used to perform calculations on numerical data
A flat file that stores records as plain sequences of text

Questions.Title: Databases vs. spreadsheets key difference
Questions.QuestionText: Which statement best describes a fundamental difference between databases and spreadsheets?
Questions.Hint: Consider the number of users and the purpose each tool is designed for.
Questions.Feedback: Spreadsheets are best for single-user analysis and ad hoc calculations, while databases are built for persistent, structured, multi-user data management.

Options.Text: Spreadsheets are designed for single-user analysis, while databases are built for multi-user structured data management
Databases can only store numerical data, while spreadsheets can store any data type
Spreadsheets enforce stricter data integrity rules than databases
Databases and spreadsheets are functionally identical but differ only in appearance

Questions.Title: Flat files compared to databases
Questions.QuestionText: How do flat files such as CSV files differ from databases?
Questions.Hint: Think about what a CSV file lacks when compared to a full database system.
Questions.Feedback: Flat files store data as simple sequences of records with no built-in structure, relationships, querying power, or integrity controls — unlike databases.

Options.Text: Flat files lack built-in structure, relationships, and integrity controls found in databases
Flat files support multi-user access better than databases do
Flat files enforce referential integrity automatically
Flat files are a type of relational database optimized for large datasets

Questions.Title: Origin of the relational model
Questions.QuestionText: Who introduced the relational model that became the foundation for most modern database systems?
Questions.Hint: This person introduced the model in 1970.
Questions.Feedback: E.F. Codd introduced the relational model in 1970, and it remains the dominant approach for structured data storage today.

Options.Text: E.F. Codd
Alan Turing
Edgar Dijkstra
Tim Berners-Lee

Questions.Title: Purpose of a primary key
Questions.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of a primary key in a relational database table?
Questions.Hint: Consider what makes each record distinguishable from all others in the same table.
Questions.Feedback: A primary key uniquely identifies each record in a table, ensuring that no two rows share the same identifier even if other data values are similar.

Options.Text: To uniquely identify each record in the table
To link a table to an external flat file
To store calculated values derived from other columns
To restrict which users can access the table

Questions.Title: Foreign key function in databases
Questions.QuestionText: What does a foreign key do in a relational database?
Questions.Hint: Think about how two separate tables can be connected to each other.
Questions.Feedback: A foreign key is a column in one table that references the primary key of another table, establishing a link between the two tables and enabling JOIN operations.

Options.Text: It references the primary key of another table to establish a link between the two tables
It generates unique sequential numbers for each new record inserted
It stores duplicate copies of data for backup purposes
It restricts a column to only accept numeric data types

Questions.Title: What referential integrity enforces
Questions.QuestionText: What does referential integrity ensure in a relational database?
Questions.Hint: Think about the relationship between foreign key values and the table they reference.
Questions.Feedback: Referential integrity ensures that foreign key values always correspond to valid primary key values in the related table, preventing orphaned or invalid references.

Options.Text: Foreign key values always correspond to valid primary key values in the related table
All columns in a table contain the same data type
Each table has at least one surrogate key assigned by the system
Duplicate rows are automatically removed from every table

Questions.Title: Natural key vs. surrogate key
Questions.QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of a surrogate key?
Questions.Hint: Consider which type of key is generated by the database system rather than derived from real-world data.
Questions.Feedback: A surrogate key is artificially generated by the database, such as an auto-incremented integer (1, 2, 3). A Social Security Number is an example of a natural key.

Options.Text: An auto-incremented integer ID assigned by the database system
A Social Security Number used to identify a person
A product barcode taken from the physical item
A passport number provided by a government agency

Questions.Title: Composite key definition and use
Questions.QuestionText: When is a composite key used in a relational database?
Questions.Hint: Think about situations where no single column can uniquely identify a record on its own.
Questions.Feedback: A composite key uses two or more columns together to uniquely identify a record when no single column alone is sufficient, such as combining StudentID and CourseID in an enrollment table.

Options.Text: When no single column can uniquely identify a record on its own
When a table needs to store data from two different databases simultaneously
When a database has more than one schema defined
When a column must store multiple values in a single cell

Questions.Title: What a database schema defines
Questions.QuestionText: What does a database schema define?
Questions.Hint: Think of a schema as a blueprint — what does a blueprint describe?
Questions.Feedback: A schema is the formal blueprint of a database that defines its tables, columns, data types, and relationships without containing the actual data itself.

Options.Text: The tables, columns, data types, and relationships that make up the database structure
The actual data records currently stored in the database
The login credentials and user permissions for database access
The backup schedule and storage location of the database files

Questions.Title: Schema vs. database instance
Questions.QuestionText: Which analogy best illustrates the difference between a database schema and a database instance?
Questions.Hint: One represents the template or plan; the other represents the actual current content.
Questions.Feedback: A schema is like a class roster template (the structure), while a database instance is like the list of students enrolled this semester (the actual data at a given moment).

Options.Text: A class roster template is the schema; the enrolled students list this semester is the instance
A class roster template is the instance; the enrolled students list is the schema
Both the template and the student list represent the schema at different times
The schema and instance are identical because one always reflects the other

Questions.Title: Most common table relationship type
Questions.QuestionText: Which type of table relationship is described as the most common in relational databases?
Questions.Hint: Consider the relationship between customers and the orders they place.
Questions.Feedback: One-to-many is the most common relationship type — for example, one customer can place many orders, but each order belongs to only one customer.

Options.Text: One-to-many
Many-to-many
One-to-one
Zero-to-many

Questions.Title: Purpose of a junction table
Questions.QuestionText: Why is a junction table used in a relational database?
Questions.Hint: Think about what happens when multiple rows in two different tables need to relate to each other.
Questions.Feedback: A junction table sits between two tables in a many-to-many relationship, storing the combinations of related rows using foreign keys from each table, often forming a composite primary key.

Options.Text: To represent a many-to-many relationship between two tables by storing combinations of related rows
To duplicate all data from two tables into a single combined table for faster retrieval
To replace the primary key of each related table with a shared composite key
To enforce one-to-one relationships between two tables in a schema

Questions.Title: Role of rows in a database table
Questions.QuestionText: What does each row in a relational database table represent?
Questions.Hint: Think about what a single entry or record looks like in a Customers table.
Questions.Feedback: Each row in a table represents a single, complete record — one specific instance of the entity the table describes, such as all the data for one individual customer.

Options.Text: A single, complete record representing one specific instance of the entity the table describes
A category of data shared by all records in the table
A data type definition applied to an entire column
A relationship link between two different tables

Questions.Title: Columns as table attributes
Questions.QuestionText: What do columns in a relational database table represent?
Questions.Hint: Consider what the column headers in a Products table like ProductID or Price describe.
Questions.Feedback: Each column in a table represents a specific attribute or characteristic shared by every record in that table, such as ProductID, ProductName, Price, and StockQuantity in a Products table.

Options.Text: Specific attributes or characteristics shared by every record in the table
Individual data records each describing one entity instance
Relationships between two separate database tables
Unique identifiers generated automatically for each row

Questions.Title: What a cell holds in a table
Questions.QuestionText: What is held at the intersection of a row and a column in a relational database table?
Questions.Hint: Think about the most granular unit of data in a table's grid structure.
Questions.Feedback: The intersection of a row and a column is called a cell, and it holds one individual data value — for example, a single email address for a specific customer.

Options.Text: One individual data value
An entire record for one entity
A list of all values in the column
A foreign key linking to another table

Questions.Title: Benefits of the relational model
Questions.QuestionText: Which of the following is a stated benefit of the relational model for data management?
Questions.Hint: Think about what problems like repeated data across tables or inaccurate records the relational model is designed to solve.
Questions.Feedback: The relational model supports efficient data management by reducing redundancy, improving data integrity, and enabling flexible querying — making it the go-to for business applications.

Options.Text: It reduces redundancy, improves data integrity, and enables flexible querying
It eliminates the need for any query language by automating all data retrieval
It stores all data in a single flat file to simplify management
It requires no schema definition, allowing data to be added in any format

Questions.Title: SQL JOIN and key relationships
Questions.QuestionText: How do SQL JOIN operations use keys when combining data from multiple tables?
Questions.Hint: Think about what connects the rows in two separate tables so that related data can be retrieved together.
Questions.Feedback: SQL JOIN operations use primary and foreign key relationships to combine rows from multiple tables, allowing analysts to retrieve related data in a single query result.

Options.Text: They use primary and foreign key relationships to combine rows from multiple tables
They copy all rows from both tables into a temporary flat file before filtering
They require both tables to have identical column names and structures
They rely on unique constraints rather than keys to match related records

Questions.Title: Schema diagram alternative name
Questions.QuestionText: What is another name for a schema diagram that visually shows tables and their connections?
Questions.Hint: Think of the acronym commonly used in database design documentation.
Questions.Feedback: A schema diagram is also called an Entity-Relationship diagram or ERD. It is a visual representation showing tables and how they connect, widely used during database design.

Options.Text: Entity-Relationship diagram (ERD)
Unified Modeling Language diagram (UML)
Data Flow Diagram (DFD)
Object-Oriented Schema (OOS)

Questions.Title: Why analysts review schemas first
Questions.QuestionText: Why is reviewing a database schema recommended before writing SQL queries?
Questions.Hint: Consider what information a schema provides that helps you write accurate queries.
Questions.Feedback: Reviewing the schema helps analysts understand all tables, columns, data types, and relationships in the database, allowing them to write fewer errors and more targeted, efficient queries.

Options.Text: It reveals all tables, columns, data types, and relationships, helping analysts write accurate and efficient queries
It automatically generates the SQL query code so no manual writing is needed
It grants additional database permissions required to run complex queries
It converts unstructured data into structured format before querying begins
